summary: Tom Mitchell's Machine Learning (McGraw-Hill, 1997) is a foundational introductory textbook presenting the core algorithms, theory, and applications of machine learning. It covers how computer programs can automatically improve through experience, drawing on statistics, AI, information theory, and other disciplines. The book targets advanced undergraduates, graduate students, and software professionals, balancing theory and practice with no prior background assumed.
bullets: Introduces machine learning as the study of algorithms that improve automatically through experience
Balances theoretical foundations (statistics, computational complexity, Bayesian analysis) with practical algorithms and traces of their operation
Highlights real applications: speech recognition, fraud detection, autonomous driving, pneumonia recovery prediction, and backgammon playing
Emphasizes the multidisciplinary nature of ML, drawing from AI, probability, control theory, neurobiology, and philosophy
Provides online datasets and code (neural nets, decision trees, Bayes classifiers) for hands-on learning
